title: Non-Western Archetype Analysis
description: Documents 10 narrative archetypes from non-Western storytelling traditions. Each archetype includes structural nodes, edge patterns, cultural context, how it differs from Western equivalents, and example works. The current 15 archetypes draw primarily from Western storytelling (Campbell, Booker, Propp); this analysis identifies patterns that are absent, only partially represented, or structurally distinct.

archetypes.name: The Wuxia Hero's Path (武侠)

structural_nodes.label: Humble/Orphaned Beginning
structural_nodes.description: The hero begins in poverty, often orphaned or displaced. Unlike the Western Hero's Journey, the origin emphasizes social injustice rather than comfortable normalcy.

structural_nodes.label: Encounter with a Master
structural_nodes.description: The hero meets a martial master who transmits both technique and moral philosophy. The mentor relationship is hierarchical and lifelong, unlike the Western mentor who gives gifts and departs.

structural_nodes.label: Cultivation and Training
structural_nodes.description: Extended period of skill development that is simultaneously physical and spiritual. The training IS the transformation, not just preparation for it.

structural_nodes.label: Jianghu Conflict
structural_nodes.description: The hero enters the jianghu (martial world) and encounters its complex politics, rivalries, and codes of honor.

structural_nodes.label: Discovery of Hidden Truth
structural_nodes.description: A secret about the hero's past, their master, or the martial world is revealed — often involving betrayal or hidden lineage.

structural_nodes.label: Confrontation with the Corrupt
structural_nodes.description: The hero faces a powerful antagonist who represents corruption of martial principles — power without righteousness.

structural_nodes.label: Withdrawal or Seclusion
structural_nodes.description: Unlike Western triumph, the wuxia hero often withdraws from the martial world, having proven their righteousness but recognizing the world's corruption cannot be fully defeated.

edge_patterns: Training precedes conflict (skill before challenge, reversing the Western pattern of challenge revealing skill)
Betrayal is structural, not incidental — the martial world's politics require it
Resolution is withdrawal rather than triumph — the hero chooses not to rule

key_differences: Training is central and extended, not a brief mentor encounter
The martial world (jianghu) is morally ambiguous, not clearly divided into ordinary/special worlds
Resolution is often withdrawal or exile, not triumphant return
The hero's moral code (义 — righteousness) is a constraint, not just a motivation
Power acquisition IS character development, not separate from it

example_works.title: The Legend of the Condor Heroes
example_works.creator: Jin Yong

archetypes.name: The Dharma Conflict (धर्मसंकट)

structural_nodes.label: Established Duty
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist occupies a defined social and moral position with clear duties (dharma). Unlike the Western 'ordinary world,' the origin is not boring — it is meaningful and ordered.

structural_nodes.label: Conflicting Duties
structural_nodes.description: Two equally valid moral obligations come into irreconcilable conflict. This is NOT a temptation (as in Tragedy) but a genuine dilemma where both options are righteous.

structural_nodes.label: Counsel and Deliberation
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist seeks counsel — from elders, gods, or scripture — but the conflict cannot be resolved by wisdom alone because both duties are valid.

structural_nodes.label: The Impossible Choice
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist must act despite the irresolvability. Any action violates one duty to fulfill another.

structural_nodes.label: Consequence of Action
structural_nodes.description: The chosen action succeeds but at devastating moral cost. The violated duty exacts its price.

structural_nodes.label: Acceptance and Karma
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist accepts the karmic consequence of their choice. Resolution is not triumph or defeat but acceptance of moral complexity.

edge_patterns: The conflict is between duties, not between good and evil
Counsel deepens the dilemma rather than resolving it
Action does not resolve the moral tension — it transforms it into consequence
Resolution is acceptance, not restoration

key_differences: The protagonist's choice is not a flaw — both options are genuinely righteous
There is no hamartia (fatal flaw) — the tragedy arises from the world's moral structure, not the character's weakness
Resolution is acceptance and karmic understanding, not catharsis through suffering
The cosmos has a moral order (dharma/karma) that the Western tragic universe lacks
No anagnorisis (recognition of error) because no error was committed — only an impossible choice

archetypes.name: The Divine Exile and Return (वनवास)

structural_nodes.label: Rightful Position
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist occupies their rightful place — often a throne, home, or social role. The origin is not ordinary but elevated.

structural_nodes.label: Unjust Exile
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist is exiled through injustice — a curse, political treachery, or divine test. The exile is NOT chosen (unlike the Hero's Journey's voluntary departure).

structural_nodes.label: Wandering in Wilderness
structural_nodes.description: Extended period of wandering that tests virtue through deprivation. The trials are about maintaining dharma under duress, not gaining new powers.

structural_nodes.label: Gathering of Allies
structural_nodes.description: The exile attracts allies who recognize the protagonist's righteousness — not recruited but drawn by moral gravity.

structural_nodes.label: Great Battle or Test
structural_nodes.description: A climactic confrontation with the force that caused the exile. The battle is cosmic in scale, not personal.

structural_nodes.label: Restoration of Order
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist returns to their rightful position, restoring cosmic and social order.

structural_nodes.label: Righteous Rule
structural_nodes.description: The returned protagonist rules with the wisdom gained in exile. The resolution establishes a golden age or moral exemplar.

edge_patterns: Exile is imposed, not chosen — the departure is involuntary
Trials maintain rather than build character — the protagonist is already righteous
Allies come to the protagonist rather than being sought
Return restores cosmic order, not just personal satisfaction

key_differences: The departure is involuntary exile, not voluntary voyage
The protagonist is already complete — exile tests rather than builds virtue
The return has cosmic significance — dharmic order restored — not just personal change
The 'other world' is the wilderness of deprivation, not a land of wonder
The resolution establishes a new order, not a return to the status quo

archetypes.name: The Trickster Cycle

structural_nodes.label: The World as Given
structural_nodes.description: The world has an established order — often unjust or rigid. The trickster exists within this order but is not constrained by it.

structural_nodes.label: The Trickster's Need
structural_nodes.description: The trickster wants something — food, status, knowledge, entertainment — and the conventional path is blocked.

structural_nodes.label: The Trick Conceived
structural_nodes.description: The trickster devises a plan that exploits the rules of the established order. The trick uses the system against itself.

structural_nodes.label: The Trick in Action
structural_nodes.description: The trickster executes the plan, which initially works but generates unexpected consequences. Each success creates a new problem.

structural_nodes.label: The Trick Backfires
structural_nodes.description: The trickster's cleverness overreaches and the trick collapses, often hilariously. The trickster suffers the consequences of their own cleverness.

structural_nodes.label: The World Changed (Accidentally)
structural_nodes.description: Despite the trickster's personal failure, the world is permanently changed by the trick. A new feature of reality exists (why the sun moves, why spiders have thin waists, why stories belong to everyone).

edge_patterns: The trick is amoral — neither good nor evil, just clever
Consequences are disproportionate to intentions — small tricks produce large changes
The trickster fails personally but succeeds cosmically
The cycle is episodic, not cumulative — the trickster doesn't learn
World-change is accidental, not intentional

key_differences: No moral lesson — the trickster doesn't learn or improve
The world is changed permanently, not restored to order
The protagonist is amoral, not sympathetic — we admire cleverness, not virtue
Episodic structure with no cumulative character arc
Cosmogonic function — explains why the world is the way it is
The trickster IS the disruption, not the victim of it

archetypes.name: The Community Restoration

structural_nodes.label: Community in Balance
structural_nodes.description: The community exists in functional harmony — not perfect, but working. The emphasis is on the collective, not an individual protagonist.

structural_nodes.label: Threat to the Community
structural_nodes.description: An external or internal threat disrupts community harmony — drought, invasion, betrayal, or curse.

structural_nodes.label: Community Deliberation
structural_nodes.description: The community gathers to discuss the threat. Leadership emerges through consensus, not individual heroism. Elders, spirit-mediums, or council members guide discussion.

structural_nodes.label: Collective Action
structural_nodes.description: The response is collective — each member contributes their unique skill or knowledge. The trials test the community's bonds, not an individual's strength.

structural_nodes.label: Ancestor/Spirit Guidance
structural_nodes.description: Ancestral spirits or natural forces provide guidance that reconnects the community to deeper truth. The revelation comes through ritual, dream, or divination.

structural_nodes.label: The Community's Sacrifice
structural_nodes.description: The community must collectively sacrifice something — comfort, tradition, or pride — to overcome the threat.

structural_nodes.label: Restored and Renewed Community
structural_nodes.description: The community is restored to harmony, often at a deeper level than before. The resolution strengthens communal bonds.

edge_patterns: Agency is collective, not individual — no single hero drives the action
Wisdom comes from ancestors and tradition, not personal discovery
Sacrifice is communal, not individual heroism
Restoration includes spiritual/ancestral reconnection, not just practical resolution

key_differences: Protagonist is the community, not an individual — fundamentally different from all 15 Western archetypes
Wisdom comes from ancestors and tradition, not mentors or personal discovery
Resolution strengthens communal bonds rather than celebrating individual transformation
Spiritual/ancestral dimension is structural, not optional — the spirit world is part of the story's mechanism
The closest Western parallel is the ensemble drama, but even that has individual focal characters

archetypes.name: The Frame-Tale Labyrinth

structural_nodes.label: The Frame Situation
structural_nodes.description: A life-or-death frame situation (Scheherazade must tell stories to survive; a king must hear wisdom to rule). The frame provides existential urgency.

structural_nodes.label: Story Begins Within Story
structural_nodes.description: The first nested story opens, which itself may contain another story. Each level of nesting creates a new world with its own logic.

structural_nodes.label: Nested Complications
structural_nodes.description: Stories within stories create moral parallels, contradictions, and resonances with the frame story. The characters in nested stories face versions of the frame character's dilemma.

structural_nodes.label: Cross-Level Insight
structural_nodes.description: A pattern emerges across story levels — the nested stories collectively reveal a truth invisible at any single level.

structural_nodes.label: Frame Resolution Threatened
structural_nodes.description: The frame situation reaches its crisis — the storyteller may run out of stories, the listener may reject the wisdom.

structural_nodes.label: Frame Transformed by Stories
structural_nodes.description: The accumulated stories have changed the frame situation. The king is transformed, the death sentence lifted, the question answered — not by a single story but by the cumulative pattern.

edge_patterns: Stories are nested, not sequential — each story contains others
Meaning emerges from the pattern across stories, not from any single narrative
The frame protagonist is transformed by listening/telling, not by acting
Time operates differently — the frame's urgency is suspended by the story-within-story structure

key_differences: No single linear arc — the structure is recursive and nested
The protagonist's primary action is storytelling/listening, not physical or moral action
Meaning is distributed across levels, not concentrated at a climax
Transformation happens to the audience (the frame character) through accumulated narrative wisdom
The Western frame story (Canterbury Tales, Decameron) is similar but typically sequential rather than truly nested

archetypes.name: The Cycle of Attachment and Release

structural_nodes.label: Worldly Attachment
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist is deeply attached to worldly things — power, love, beauty, knowledge. Unlike Western origins, the attachment is not presented as 'ordinary' — it is the problem.

structural_nodes.label: Suffering Manifests
structural_nodes.description: The attachment produces suffering — loss, disappointment, or the realization that the desired thing cannot satisfy. This is not an external disruption but an inherent consequence of attachment.

structural_nodes.label: Attempts to Satisfy Desire
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist tries harder to fulfill the attachment — acquiring more, holding tighter, seeking deeper — but each attempt deepens the suffering.

structural_nodes.label: Maximum Suffering
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist reaches the nadir — total loss, complete disillusionment, or the recognition that desire itself is the source of pain.

structural_nodes.label: Insight into Impermanence
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist perceives the impermanence of all things. This is not intellectual knowledge but experiential wisdom — feeling the truth of transience.

structural_nodes.label: Release (Liberation)
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist releases attachment and achieves a form of liberation — not triumph or achievement but freedom from the cycle of desire and suffering.

edge_patterns: The disruption is internal, not external — suffering arises from attachment itself
Escalation is paradoxical — trying harder makes things worse
The revelation is not new information but a shift in perception
Resolution is subtraction (letting go), not addition (gaining something)

key_differences: No moral flaw — the protagonist's attachment is universal human nature, not a character deficiency
Resolution is release/liberation, not renewed engagement with the world
The 'transformation' is subtraction (letting go) not addition (gaining virtue)
The world doesn't change — only the protagonist's relationship to it changes
There is no atonement — nothing was done wrong; attachment is the human condition

example_works.title: Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter... and Spring
example_works.creator: Kim Ki-duk

archetypes.name: The Vision Quest / Spirit Journey

structural_nodes.label: Community Membership
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist is part of a community but has not yet found their specific role or purpose. The origin is belonging without yet knowing what you belong as.

structural_nodes.label: Preparation and Fasting
structural_nodes.description: The community prepares the seeker through ritual — fasting, purification, instruction from elders. The preparation is communal, not individual.

structural_nodes.label: Departure into Wilderness
structural_nodes.description: The seeker departs alone into wilderness — mountains, desert, or forest. The isolation is deliberate and structured, not accidental.

structural_nodes.label: Solitude and Deprivation
structural_nodes.description: The seeker endures hunger, cold, fear, and solitude. The trial is not a battle but an endurance of absence — waiting for what comes.

structural_nodes.label: The Vision
structural_nodes.description: A vision comes — an animal spirit, ancestor, or natural force reveals the seeker's purpose, name, or medicine. The vision is received, not achieved.

structural_nodes.label: Return to Community with Purpose
structural_nodes.description: The seeker returns to the community and shares the vision with elders. The community helps interpret the vision and the seeker assumes their role.

edge_patterns: Preparation is communal; the quest is solitary; interpretation is communal
The revelation is received (passive), not achieved (active)
Power/purpose comes from the spirit world, not from the self
The return is to a specific social role, not to general 'changed person' status

key_differences: The quest is for identity/purpose, not for an external goal or treasure
The vision is received through waiting, not achieved through action
The community frames both departure and return — the quest is a communal process with an individual phase
There is no antagonist — the trial is endurance, not combat
The 'reward' is a specific social role and relationship to the spirit world, not a general transformation

archetypes.name: The Shahnameh Heroic Cycle

structural_nodes.label: Noble Birth with Omen
structural_nodes.description: The hero is born with extraordinary signs — supernatural strength, prophetic destiny, or divine parentage. The origin signals that this life is fated.

structural_nodes.label: Seven Labors / Tests
structural_nodes.description: The hero undergoes a structured series of trials — often seven — that prove their worthiness. Each labor tests a different virtue: strength, cunning, piety, endurance.

structural_nodes.label: Service to the Throne
structural_nodes.description: The hero commits to serve the kingdom — but the throne may not deserve the hero's loyalty. The tension between heroic virtue and royal corruption is central.

structural_nodes.label: Betrayal by the Throne
structural_nodes.description: The king or court betrays the hero through jealousy, manipulation, or political expedience. The hero's greatest threat comes from those they serve.

structural_nodes.label: Tragic Confrontation
structural_nodes.description: The hero is forced into a confrontation they cannot win morally — often unknowingly fighting their own kin, or choosing between loyalty and justice.

structural_nodes.label: The Hero's Lament
structural_nodes.description: The hero suffers irreversible loss — killing a son unknowingly, losing a homeland, or being exiled. The lament is public, ritualized, and cosmic in significance.

structural_nodes.label: Enduring Legend
structural_nodes.description: The hero's story becomes a legend that outlasts the corrupt kingdom. The individual is mortal; the story is immortal.

edge_patterns: Destiny is announced at birth, not discovered through journey
Trials are structured and numbered (seven labors), not open-ended
The betrayal comes from within (the court), not from without (a monster)
The hero's tragedy is caused by loyalty to an unworthy throne
Resolution is in legend, not in life — the story outlasts the hero

key_differences: The hero's flaw is excessive loyalty, not pride or ambition
Betrayal comes from the institution the hero serves, not from personal relationships
The trials are formal and structured (seven labors), not organic journey-trials
Resolution is in cultural memory — the story itself is the redemption
The hero is tragic but not diminished — they remain noble throughout, unlike Western tragic heroes who fall morally

archetypes.name: The Ancestor-Guided Journey

structural_nodes.label: Living in the Present
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist lives in the present but is disconnected from ancestral wisdom — through modernity, exile, or ignorance.

structural_nodes.label: Crisis That Present Knowledge Cannot Solve
structural_nodes.description: A crisis arises that modern/present knowledge and skills cannot resolve. The living world's resources are insufficient.

structural_nodes.label: Ancestral Contact
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist makes contact with ancestors — through dream, ritual, shrine, or a living elder who carries ancestral knowledge. The ancestors speak.

structural_nodes.label: Learning the Old Ways
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist must learn or re-learn traditional practices, values, or knowledge that have been forgotten or abandoned.

structural_nodes.label: Conflict Between Old and New
structural_nodes.description: The ancestral wisdom conflicts with modern circumstances. The protagonist must find a way to honor the ancestors while living in the present.

structural_nodes.label: Synthesis of Past and Present
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist integrates ancestral wisdom with present reality, creating something that honors both.

structural_nodes.label: Reconnected Community
structural_nodes.description: The protagonist's synthesis reconnects their community to its ancestral roots while remaining viable in the present.

edge_patterns: Wisdom flows backward in time — from ancestors to descendants
The protagonist's task is reconnection, not discovery
The crisis is a clash between temporal modes (past vs. present), not between good and evil
Resolution requires synthesis, not victory of one mode over another

key_differences: The 'fallen state' is disconnection from tradition, not personal moral failure
Wisdom comes from ancestors, not from personal suffering or insight
The resolution is communal reconnection, not individual renewal
The past is a resource, not a burden — ancestral wisdom is correct, not outdated
The protagonist's task is to bridge temporalities, not to transform personally

structural_observations: The most significant structural difference is the role of the community: Western archetypes center individual protagonists, while African and Indigenous archetypes often center the community as protagonist.

Resolution types diverge sharply: Western archetypes resolve in triumph, restoration, or catharsis; non-Western archetypes more commonly resolve in acceptance (Buddhist), withdrawal (wuxia), communal reconnection (African), or legendary persistence (Persian).

The mentor relationship differs: Western mentors give gifts and depart; South Asian gurus transmit lifelong discipline; East Asian masters embed moral philosophy in physical training; African elders carry ancestral wisdom.

Moral structure varies: Western archetypes typically use good-vs-evil or flaw-vs-virtue; South Asian archetypes use duty-vs-duty; Buddhist archetypes use attachment-vs-release; the trickster cycle is amoral entirely.
Time structure varies: the Frame-Tale Labyrinth and the Trickster Cycle are episodic or recursive, unlike the Western assumption of linear progression through discrete phases.
